channel.title: 今日のギャグフィード
channel.description: Google Gemini APIによって毎日自動生成される今日のギャグフィードです。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 110
item.description: 寿司屋で「大将、これ美味しいね！」って言ったら、「ええ、イカしてますから！」だって。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 494
item.description: 「コアラって、なんでいつも寝てるの？」 「そりゃあ、"コアラ"いからさ！」

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 304
item.description: 駅員さんが「えっ？」って聞き返すのは、なんでだと思う？…そこが『駅(えき)』だからね！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 1780
item.description:
[THOUGHT PROCESS] The user wants one interesting "Oyaji Gag" (Dad Joke) in Japanese. The output should only be the text of the gag, with no extra explanation, intro, or greeting.
Let's brainstorm some common dad joke types and try to make a Japanese pun.
1. **Wordplay/Homophones:** This is the most common type for Japanese dad jokes. * Example: "布団が吹っ飛んだ！" (Futon ga futtOnda! - The futon flew away!) - pun on "futon" and "futto-nda". * Example: "パンはパンでも食べられないパンはなーんだ？ フライパン！" (Pan wa pan demo taberarenai pan wa na~nda? Furaipan! - What kind of bread can't you eat? A frying pan!) - pun on "pan" (bread) and "furaipan" (frying pan).
2. **Situational Puns:** Less common but can work.
Let's aim for a classic wordplay pun.
Consider common objects or situations.
* **Food:** * Sushi: "この寿司、ネタが新鮮でネターくない！" (Kono sushi, neta ga shinsen de neta-kunai! - This sushi, the topping is fresh and not... sleeping!) - pun on "neta" (sushi topping) and "neta" (past tense of neru - to sleep). A bit weak. * Tofu: "豆腐は腐るけど、幸福は腐らないね！" (Tōfu wa kusaru kedo, kōfuku wa kusaranai ne! - Tofu spoils, but happiness doesn't!) - pun on "fuku" in tofu and "kōfuku". A bit preachy. * Rice: "ご飯食べた？ ご飯を食べるのはご飯だよ！" (Gohan tabeta? Gohan wo taberu no wa gohan da yo! - Did you eat rice? Eating rice is rice!) - weak.
* **Animals:** * Cat (Neko): "猫が寝込んだ！" (Neko ga nekonda! - The cat fell sick!) - pun on "neko" and "nekonda". A bit too simple. * Dog (Inu): "犬はワンワンと吠えるけど、犬を飼うとワンダフルだね！" (Inu wa wanwan to hoeru kedo, inu wo kau to wonderful da ne! - Dogs bark "wanwan," but owning a dog is wonderful!) - English pun. Maybe not ideal for a *purely Japanese* dad joke.
* **Everyday objects/actions:** * Phone (Denwa): "電話でんわ？ 出んわ！" (Denwa denwa? Denwa! - Phone phone? Not coming out/answering!) - pun on "denwa" (phone) and "denwa" (I'm not coming out / answering). This is a classic and good. Let's refine it. * Q: "もしもし、電話でんわ？" (Moshimoshi, denwa denwa? - Hello, is that the phone?) * A: "出んわ！" (Denwa! - I'm not answering!) * Or even simpler: * "電話、出んわ！" (Denwa, denwa! - The phone, I'm not answering!) - This implies the phone is ringing but the person isn't picking up. It's a bit self-referential which makes it typical dad joke.
Let's try another one with wordplay, maybe slightly more complex but still easy to get.
* **Shoes (Kutsu):** * "靴を履くのは得意だけど、靴を脱ぐのは苦手なんだ。だって、靴脱いだら臭いんだもん！" (Kutsu wo haku no wa tokui dakedo, kutsu wo nugu no wa nigate nanda. Datte, kutsu nuidara kusai n da mon! - I'm good at putting on shoes, but bad at taking them off. Because when I take off my shoes, they stink!) - "Kutsu" (shoes) and "kusai" (stinky). This is okay, a bit long for a *single line gag*.
* **Train (Densha):** * "電車が来ないな〜。なんでかって？ まだ『発車』してないからね！" (Densha ga konai na~. Nande ka tte? Mada 'hassha' shitenai kara ne! - The train isn't coming~. Why? Because it hasn't 'departed' yet!) - This isn't a pun, more a literal statement presented as a joke. Not quite an oyaji gag. * "電車でんわ？" (Densha denwa? - Is it a train?) No, that doesn't work.
Let's go back to the "phone" one. It's a classic for a reason.
"電話に出んわ！" (Denwa ni denwa!) This translates to: "I won't answer the phone!" but uses the archaic/dialectal "denwa" (出んわ) for "denai" (出ない). The pun is on "denwa" (電話 - phone) and "denwa" (出んわ - won't come out/answer).
It's concise and fits the criteria.
Another option: "このパン、焼きたてだね！ いや、まだ焼けーてないかも？" (Kono pan, yakitate da ne! Iya, mada yake-tenai kamo? - This bread is freshly baked! No, maybe it hasn't baked yet?) Pun on 焼きたて (yakitate - freshly baked) and 焼けてない (yaketenai - not baked). Not as strong.
Let's stick with the phone one, it's a solid choice. "電話に出んわ！"
Or a slight variation: "電話が鳴ってるよ！ …でも、出んわ！" (Denwa ga natteru yo! ...Demo, denwa! - The phone's ringing! ...But, I won't answer!) - This gives a bit more context.
The user asked for *one* gag. "電話に出んわ！" is perfect.
Let's double-check the requirements: - One interesting "Oyaji Gag" - In Japanese - Output only the text of the gag - No extra explanation, intro, greeting.
"電話に出んわ！" meets all criteria. It's a pun where 電話 (denwa - phone) is used, and 出んわ (denwa - won't answer) is used. It's a common and simple dad joke in Japanese.
Final check on "interesting". Oyaji gags are by nature often groan-worthy rather than genuinely hilarious, but the cleverness of the pun is what makes them "interesting" in their own way for the genre. This one is a classic example.

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 849
item.description: 今日のランチ、カレーライスだったんだけどさ、食べたら「カレ〜い！」って叫びたくなったよ。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 550
item.description: パンはパンでも食べられないパンはなーんだ？ フライパン！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 413
item.description: 「お父さん、今日のご飯はカレーだよ！」「お、華麗だね！」

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 198
item.description: 「ねぇ、あの鳥なんて名前？」 「さぁ…『トリ』あえず、わかんないな。」

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-05) 403
item.description: 「お茶ください！」 「『お茶』っと待ってください！」

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-04) 1408
item.description: 冷蔵庫に象がいたゾウ！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-04) 805
item.description: 最近、寒くて布団から出られないんだ。 わかるよ、俺も寒くてコタツ（答えず）られないよ。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-04) 127
item.description: 今日のランチ、蕎麦屋で隣のオヤジが「かけそばください」って言ったら、店員さんが「かしこまりました。かけそばですね？」って聞き返してた。なんでかって？「かけ」と「蕎麦」を「かけ」てなかったからさ！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-04) 509
item.description: お寿司屋さんでバイト始めたら、店長に「ネタがいいね！」って褒められたよ。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-04) 1904
item.description:
[THINK] The user wants one interesting "oyaji gag" (dad joke) in Japanese. I need to generate just the text of the gag, no extra explanations or greetings.
Let's brainstorm some Japanese dad joke styles: 1. **Puns/Wordplay (ダジャレ):** This is the most common type of oyaji gag. Often involves a word that sounds like another, or a phrase that can be interpreted differently. 2. **Situational/Observational (状況依存):** Less common for short, standalone gags, but possible. 3. **Twisted common phrases (ことわざ・慣用句のひねり):** Taking a well-known phrase and giving it a silly twist.
I'll aim for a pun/wordplay one as it's the most classic "oyaji gag" style and usually fits the "interesting" criteria for a dad joke.
Let's try some themes: * Food: "布団が吹っ飛んだ" (futon ga futtunda - the futon blew away, sounds like "futon ga futtonda") - This is a classic but maybe too common. * Animals: * Everyday objects:
How about something related to a common thing people might encounter? A traffic light (信号 - shingou). What if it's broken? "信号が壊れた！" (Shingou ga kowareta!) What does it do? "点滅してる" (tenmetsu shiteru - it's blinking). What if it's blinking too much? "信号が点滅してるんだけど、これって「ちょっと待って」ってこと？" (Shingou ga tenmetsu shiteru n'dakedo, kore tte "chotto matte" tte koto?) This isn't a pun.
Let's go for a classic pun structure. Something about "いる" (iru - to be/exist) or "いる" (iru - to need).
How about something with a plant? "サボテン" (saboten - cactus) What's a pun with saboten? "サボテンが喋った！" (Saboten ga shabetta! - The cactus spoke!) "喋っ展" (shabetten - a speaking exhibition/display) - This is a bit forced.
Let's try a different approach. A question and answer format that reveals the pun.
Q: パンはパンでも、食べられないパンはなーんだ？ (Pan wa pan demo, taberarenai pan wa naa-nda?) (What kind of bread is bread, but you can't eat?) A: フライパン！ (Furai pan! - Frying pan!) This is a very classic dad joke. It fits the criteria. It's simple, well-known, and definitely an "oyaji gag".
Let's try to make one that might be *slightly* less common but still easy to understand.
How about something with "布団" (futon - Japanese mattress/bedding)? "布団が吹っ飛んだ" (Futon ga futtunda!) - The futon flew away! (This is a famous one, often taught to children to illustrate onomatopoeia, but also a classic oyaji gag).
Let's try to generate one based on a common scenario. What if someone asks for a gift? "プレゼント" (present)
How about a food item? "ラーメン" (ramen) "ラーメンを食べている時に、誰かに「食べてる」って聞かれたら、どう答える？" (Ramen wo tabeteiru toki ni, dareka ni "Tabeteru?" tte kikaretara, dou kotaeru?) (When you're eating ramen, and someone asks "Are you eating?", how do you reply?) "「ラーメン、食べてるわ！」って言わないで、「ラーメン、**いらん！**」って答えるんだよ！" ("Ramen, tabeteru wa!" tte iwanai de, "Ramen, **iran!**" tte kotaerunda yo!) (Don't say "I'm eating ramen!", say "**I don't need** ramen!") The pun is "いらん" (iran - I don't need) vs "いるんですか" (irundesu ka - are you eating it/do you need it?) for the ramen being "eaten." This might be a bit convoluted.
Let's stick to a simple, direct pun.
How about "イルカ" (iruka - dolphin)? "イルカが苦手な食べ物は何？" (Iruka ga nigate na tabemono wa nani?) (What food do dolphins dislike?) "イルカが嫌いな食べ物は、ワカメじゃなくて、**イルカに食われる**ワカメだよ！" (This is not a pun and not very good).
Let's restart with the pun strategy. A word that sounds like something else.
"コーヒー" (coffee) "ねぇ、このコーヒー、どこで買ったの？" (Nee, kono coffee, doko de katta no?) - Hey, where did you buy this coffee? "うちの奥さんが、**豆**から入れてくれたんだよ！" (Uchi no okusan ga, **mame** kara irete kureta n'da yo!) - My wife made it for me from **beans**! (The joke here is "豆から" (mame kara - from beans) sounds like "真面目" (majime - serious/diligent), implying she made it diligently, but it's literally "from beans." This is a bit weak as a pun.)
Okay, let's try a classic "what did X say to Y?" or "why did X do Y?" type of pun.
What did the *door* say to the *wall*? "ドアが壁にぶつかって言ったセリフは？" "ごめんなさい！" (Gomen nasai! - I'm sorry!) - Too simple.
How about an object that has two meanings or sounds like another word?
"お茶碗" (o-chawan - rice bowl) "お茶碗が割れちゃった時、なんでみんなが残念がるの？" (Ochawan ga warechatta toki, nande minna ga zannen garu no?) (When a rice bowl breaks, why does everyone feel disappointed?) "だって、**ちゃわん**（茶碗）がないと、ご飯が食べられちゃわん（食べられちゃわない）から！" (Datte, **chawan** ga nai to, gohan ga taberarecha**wan**ai kara!) (Because if you don't have a bowl (chawan), you can't eat rice (taberarecha-wan-ai, where "wan" sounds like the "wan" in chawan and is part of a negative contraction). This is a good pun, but a bit long to explain and maybe relies on a slightly less common contraction.)
Let's aim for a universally understandable pun in Japanese.
"コアラ" (koala) "コアラはなんでいつも寝てるの？" (Koara wa nande itsumo neteru no?) (Why are koalas always sleeping?) "だって、木の上で**コアラ**（子アール）から！" (Datte, ki no ue de **koala** (ko-aru) kara!) This one puns "コアラ" (koara) with "木の上で子（こ）ある" (ki no ue de ko aru - because there's a child on the tree/because it's a child on the tree). This is a classic, relatively simple, and good oyaji gag. The "子アール" part is a bit of a stretch but that's typical of oyaji gags.
Let's try another one. "パンはパンでも、空を飛ぶパンはなーんだ？" (Pan wa pan demo, sora wo tobu pan wa naa-nda?) (What kind of bread is bread, but flies in the sky?) "ヘリコプターン！" (Herikoputaan!) - Helicopter! (Pun on "ヘリコプター" (herikoputa) and "パン" (pan)). This is good. It's a classic format, easy to understand, and plays on the sound of "pan".
I'll go with the "パン" joke because it's concise and a very typical oyaji gag structure.
The user asked for "今日の面白いオヤジギャグを1つ日本語で作成してください。" I need to output *only* the text of the gag.
Gag: 「パンはパンでも、空を飛ぶパンはなーんだ？」 「ヘリコプターン！」
This fits all criteria. - One gag. - In Japanese. - Oyaji gag style. - Output is only the text. - No extra info.
